meta.title: Context update causes rerender storm
meta.summary: One big shared context holds too much fast-changing state, so typing in one place rerenders parts of the page that did not actually change.
signals: Typing rerenders parts of the page that did not change
One context update rerenders lots of components
The provider creates a new value object on every update

context.symptom: Typing in a filter field causes chart legends, notification badges, and sidebar widgets to rerender even though their visible data did not change.
context.userImpact: The page feels heavy, and low-end machines stutter during interactions that should be local.
context.environment: A single app-wide React context provides auth, theme, notifications, filters, drafts, and callbacks in one object.

evidence.title: Profiler result
evidence.body: One context update rerenders 47 components, and most of them end up showing the same UI as before.

evidence.title: Provider value
Observed pattern
evidence.body: Even consumers that only read theme rerender when draft text changes.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: Why is so much of the page rerendering?
stages.helperText: Look for the one thing forcing lots of components to update together.

options.label: Maybe the page just needs React.memo in more places.
options.feedback: Memo can help with props, but it does not stop a context update from reaching every consumer.

options.label: Maybe React should compare the whole context object for us, and this is mainly a framework issue.
options.feedback: React does not do that for context. The app needs better state boundaries.

options.label: One big context value changes all the time, so every consumer rerenders together.
options.feedback: Correct. The provider is too broad, and it keeps creating a new value that wakes up every consumer.

options.label: The main problem is browser layout work, not React rerenders.
options.feedback: Layout can matter, but the profiler already shows the first problem: too many React rerenders.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: What would you check first? Put the best three at the top.
stages.helperText: First prove where the broad rerender starts.

candidates.label: Profile the interaction and see which consumers rerender after the provider updates.
Check how big the provider value is and whether it becomes a new object on unrelated updates.
Confirm that components reading stable data still rerender because they subscribe to the giant context.
Wrap everything in React.memo before you confirm where the rerenders start.
Change the CSS framework to reduce rerenders.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes should actually be in the fix?
stages.helperText: Pick the changes that reduce unnecessary rerenders, not just hide them.

options.label: Rely only on React.memo around consumers and hope that stops the rerenders.
options.feedback: Memo alone does not fix a giant context.

options.label: Split the giant provider into smaller contexts based on what changes together.
options.feedback: Correct. Smaller contexts create smaller rerender boundaries.

options.label: Move fast-changing shared state to a store where components subscribe only to the piece they use.
options.feedback: Correct. Selector-based subscriptions keep unrelated components asleep.

options.label: Keep the provider value and callbacks stable anywhere you still use context.
options.feedback: Correct. Stable identities matter, though they are not enough alone if the provider stays too broad.

options.label: Deep-compare the whole provider value on every render and hope React skips updates.
options.feedback: Deep comparison is expensive and still misses the structural issue of overly broad subscription scope.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: How would you make sure this does not come back?
stages.helperText: Pick a guard that checks how far rerenders spread.

options.label: Add a render-count or profiler-based check around a component that should stay stable during draft updates.
options.feedback: Correct. This guards the exact 'too many parts of the page rerender' problem that hurt the interaction.

options.label: Open the profiler once in a while before big releases and trust that the architecture is still fine.
options.feedback: Manual profiling helps, but it is too weak as the primary guardrail.

options.label: Track API latency because server speed affects typing responsiveness.
options.feedback: That does not guard the local rerender scope issue.

options.label: Stop adding shared state to the page so the context can never grow again.
options.feedback: That blocks product work instead of enforcing healthy state boundaries.

scoreBands.label: You noticed slowness, but not why it spread so widely
scoreBands.summary: The page was rerendering too much, but the provider boundaries were still the real problem.

scoreBands.label: Right direction, incomplete structural fix
scoreBands.summary: You identified context as the problem, but one step still leaned on surface-level fixes.

scoreBands.label: You fixed the rerender boundary problem
scoreBands.summary: You diagnosed the giant provider, chose structural fixes, and guarded rerender scope directly.

idealRunbook: Profile the interaction and count how many consumers rerender.
Check how broad the provider is and when it creates a new value object.
Reduce the subscription scope with smaller contexts or selectors.
Protect the fix with a rerender-scope regression check.

teachingBlocks.title: Interview shortcut
teachingBlocks.text: When context performance comes up, talk about update boundaries first. Memoization is secondary.

teachingBlocks.title: Better answer framing
items: Explain why stable parts of the page still rerender.
Describe how you would split the state or use selectors.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If you kept context, what would make you split it into two providers instead of moving to a store?
